items.q: How does the Brundtland Report define sustainable development?
items.correct: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as 'no growth'. The Brundtland definition explicitly balances development AND environmental concerns - it's about INTERGENERATIONAL equity, not freezing change.

items.q: How does fertiliser runoff cause aquatic dead zones?
items.correct: Excess nutrients fuel algal blooms; when algae die, decomposition depletes water oxygen.
items.random_wrong: Fertiliser makes the water too acidic.
items.why: Students assume direct toxicity. Eutrophication is INDIRECT - nutrients cause excess plant growth, then decomposers consume oxygen breaking it down, suffocating fish and other aerobic organisms.

items.correct: Dissolved CO2 forms carbonic acid, lowering ocean pH.
items.misconception: Acid rain falling on the ocean.
items.why: Students think acidification is direct pollution. The main driver is atmospheric CO2 dissolving in seawater - an inevitable result of higher atmospheric concentrations, threatening shell-forming organisms.
The answer examiners want is: Glass. “Paper” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly how many times can paper be recycled?
items.why: Correct answer: 7. “100” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: By what % does recycling aluminium cut energy?
items.why: Exact answer: 95. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
